subject: Chemistry
title: Vantablack: The Darkest Material
description: A quick win: understand Vantablack: The Darkest Material.

hook.text: Vantablack absorbs 99.965% of visible light. Objects coated in it look like holes in reality.

buildup.text: It's made of carbon nanotubes standing vertically like a forest. Light enters between them and bounces until absorbed.

discovery.text: Each nanotube is 20 nanometers wide—3,500 times thinner than a human hair. The forest traps photons completely.

twist.text: Artist Anish Kapoor got exclusive art rights to Vantablack. Other artists were furious and created rival ultra-blacks.

climax.text: Ultra-black materials are used in telescopes to reduce stray light, helping capture images of distant galaxies.

punchline.text: Carbon nanotubes trap nearly all light. The darkest surface humans have ever made.

quiz.question: How does Vantablack absorb nearly all light?
options: Carbon nanotubes stand like a forest, trapping light as it bounces between them
A chemical coating converts light into heat instantly
Black pigment molecules are packed at maximum density
